channel.title: NYT > Business
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Markets in Asia Plummet as Energy Fears Offset A.I. Optimism
item.description: Stocks in Asia sank on Wednesday, led by sharp declines in South Korea, as the war in Iran raised concerns about spiraling energy costs.
item.dc:creator: River Akira Davis and Meaghan Tobin
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Currency dealers monitor exchange rates as an electronic screen shows South Korea’s benchmark stock index (KOSPI) and the Korean Securities Dealers Automated Quotations (KOSDAQ) in a foreign exchange dealing room at the Hana Bank headquarters in Seoul on Wednesday.

item.title: 9 Million Indians Live in the Persian Gulf. The Money They Send Home Is a Lifeline.
item.description: A huge number of Indians spend their lives hopping across the Arabian Sea — remitting billions of dollars a year, supporting their families and the Indian economy.
item.dc:creator: Alex Travelli
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Indian laborers who take up positions in construction, transportation and home care to send money back home.

item.title: Vail Resorts Reduces the Price of Its Epic Pass for Gen Z Skiers
item.description: Seeming to acknowledge critics’ complaints about the high cost of snow sports, the company is cutting the price of its 2026-2027 Epic Passes for younger skiers and snowboarders.
item.dc:creator: Claire Fahy
item.media:description: Younger skiers and snowboarders, like this group at Keystone, in Colorado, will pay less for next season’s Epic Passes.

item.title: Target to Spend Billions to Refresh Stores, and Hire More Workers
item.description: The new C.E.O. said the company would focus on home décor, apparel and pop culture trends in an effort to reverse years of sales declines.
item.dc:creator: Kim Bhasin
item.media:description: Target has struggled over the past three years as it has faced strong competition from rivals.

item.title: How Anonymous Bettors Cashed In on the Iran Strike, Just Hours Before It Happened
item.description: Polymarket users placed hundreds of bets of at least $1,000 predicting an imminent American strike, raising concerns about insider trading.
item.dc:creator: Amy Fan
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Monterey Bay Aquarium Seafood Watch Provide Sustainable Recommendations
item.description: Seafood Watch, long a source of advice on sustainable choices, is working to get restaurants to adopt its color-coded recommendations.
item.dc:creator: Kim Severson
item.media:credit: Erin Brethauer for The New York Times
item.media:description: William Dissen, the owner of the Market Place Restaurant in Asheville, N.C., serves farmed trout from a local fishery.

item.title: An Epstein Associate Steps Down From Japanese Government Tech Project
item.description: Joichi Ito’s involvement in a publicly funded Japanese initiative had come under scrutiny after new details revealed his close relationship with Jeffrey Epstein.
item.dc:creator: River Akira Davis and Hisako Ueno
item.media:description: Joichi Ito is leaving the Global Startup Campus Initiative after renewed scrutiny over his past ties to the convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.

item.title: Big Lenders’ Risky Loans Are Rattling Wall Street
item.description: Lending troubles at Blue Owl Capital and other so-called private credit behemoths are setting off fears of a “bank run,” as one hedge fund put it.
item.dc:creator: Rob Copeland and Maureen Farrell
item.media:description: Doug Ostrover, a founder of Blue Owl Capital. Investors are worried that colossal “private credit” lenders like Blue Owl have been too optimistic in their assessments of multiyear, privately traded loans tied to risky companies and industries.

item.title: Trump Administration, in Reversal, Tries to Continue Fight Against Law Firms
item.description: The administration told a court on Monday that it was abandoning its defense of executive orders targeting the firms. But on Tuesday, the Justice Department abruptly changed its position.
item.dc:creator: Michael S. Schmidt, Jonah E. Bromwich and Devlin Barrett
item.category: Wilmer Cutler Pickering Hale and Dorr LLP
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Justice Department indicated it would continue defending the orders in an email to the law firms on Tuesday morning.

item.title: War-Induced Inflation Spike Looms Over Europe’s Economic Recovery
item.description: For months, the region has been encouraged by low inflation and better-than-expected economic growth. A disruption to energy supplies from the Middle East could knock it off course.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Violette Franchi for The New York Times
item.media:description: The European Union imports a majority of its oil and gas, making it vulnerable to volatility in global prices.

item.title: MrBeast Is Getting Into Financial Services With His Step App. Parents, Pay Attention.
item.description: Beast Industries has acquired a banklike app for young people and could eventually offer a variety of financial services, including crypto.
item.dc:creator: Tara Siegel Bernard and Ron Lieber
item.media:credit: Thea Traff for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jimmy Donaldson, 27, better known as MrBeast, has a large following of teenagers and young adults. He has said he wants to provide them with the financial basics he lacked when he was younger.

item.title: Oil and Natural Gas Prices Keep Climbing as Iran Attacks Escalate
item.description: Energy prices continued to rise on Tuesday as attacks by the United States and Israel on Iran intensified, along with the country’s response.
item.dc:creator: Kailyn Rhone
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Drivers queuing at a gas station in Los Angeles on Monday. The price of gas was up by about 11 cents a gallon on Tuesday morning.

item.title: Has Your Business Been Affected by Immigration Policies? We Want to Hear From You.
item.description: The New York Times is looking to talk to business owners whose work forces are changing because of immigration policies.
item.dc:creator: Madeleine Ngo
item.media:description: Border Patrol agents at a gas station in Minneapolis in January.

item.title: Trump’s ‘Whatever It Takes’ Rattles Global Markets
item.description: President Trump has signaled that he is open to the idea of a prolonged military conflict with Iran. Investors are spooked by that scenario.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly and Brian O’Keefe
item.media:credit: 2026 Planet Labs PBC/Agence France-Presse via Getty Images
item.media:description: A satellite image taken after an explosion along the Strait of Hormuz. Market veterans and businesses are assessing the effects of the war in the Middle East.

item.title: Tariffs Confound Small Businesses Again
item.description: Chocolate makers and fig-paste importers are facing a tangle of unknowns, including whether to seek refunds for tariffs invalidated by the Supreme Court.
item.dc:creator: Sydney Ember and Ana Swanson
item.media:credit: Kendrick Brinson for The New York Times
item.media:description: “The level of uncertainty is crazy,” Matt Weyandt, a founder of Xocolatl, said of the tariff changes.

item.title: Ford and G.M. Face a Dilemma as China Excels in Electric Vehicles
item.description: General Motors, Ford and other established automakers risk becoming relics if they don’t catch up to Chinese carmakers and technology companies in electric vehicles and self-driving cars.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.media:credit: Emily Elconin for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Ford Motor factory in Wayne, Mich. U.S. automakers are facing difficult choices.

item.title: Global Economy Is Facing the Prospect of Another Profound Shock
item.description: A protracted conflict in the Middle East risks a spike in energy prices and broader inflation.
item.dc:creator: Peter S. Goodman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Firefighters at the Ras Tanura oil refinery in Saudi Arabia on Monday, after a drone attack. The Middle East is the source of 30 percent of the world’s oil.

item.title: Trump Says He Will End Boycott of White House Correspondents’ Dinner
item.description: The mentalist Oz Pearlman is this year’s speaker. “Hopefully, it will be something very Special,” the president wrote on Truth Social.
item.dc:creator: Shawn McCreesh and Michael M. Grynbaum
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Marine Corps Band playing at the White House Correspondents’ Dinner in Washington in 2024.

item.title: Trump Administration Abandons Efforts to Impose Executive Orders on Law Firms
item.description: The move amounts to a surrender in a clash that has led many law firms to submit to the president rather than face the threat of his executive orders.
item.dc:creator: Jonah E. Bromwich and Michael S. Schmidt
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Justice Department told the U.S. Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia on Monday that it was no longer interested in pursuing the cases contesting executive orders barring law firms from government business.

item.title: Gas Prices Begin to Climb, Trailing Surge in Crude Oil
item.description: A prolonged conflict in the Middle East could push prices even higher, as the busy driving season approaches.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The average price nationally for a gallon of unleaded gas is $3.11, the highest since mid-October.

item.title: They’re Hiring at U.S.A.I.D. Just Not Anyone Who Worked There.
item.description: The Trump administration said in a memo it wanted to “avoid the risk of impaired objectivity” by hiring former staff members to wind down operations at the U.S. Agency for International Development.
item.dc:creator: Eileen Sullivan
item.category: United States Agency for International Development
item.media:credit: Carolyn Van Houten for The New York Times
item.media:description: Former staff members and supporters of the U.S. Agency for International Development held a rally last month in Washington to mark one year since the agency was largely dismantled.

item.title: Travelers Stuck in Dubai Face ‘Chaos and Confusion’ Amid Flight Cancellations
item.description: The Emirates’ reputation as a safe destination in a volatile area was put to a brutal test in recent days as Iran, retaliating against U.S.-Israeli attacks, targeted the region with missiles and drones.
item.dc:creator: Ceylan Yeğinsu, Omnia Al Desoukie and Christine Chung
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Grounded airplanes at Dubai International Airport on Sunday. The weekend airstrikes forced the closure of airports in Dubai, Abu Dhabi and Doha, Qatar. A limited number of departures were scheduled on Monday at the Dubai airport.

item.title: Natural Gas Prices Surge After Attacks Hit Facilities in Qatar
item.description: Qatar said it had halted production of liquefied natural gas, forcing buyers to compete for supplies elsewhere.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A liquefied natural gas production facility in Qatar. The country exports L.N.G. via the Strait of Hormuz, which is a vital shipping channel for oil and other goods.

item.title: The Unexpected Winners From Trump’s New Global Tariff
item.description: The president’s flat 10 percent tariff is most beneficial to nations that previously faced the highest rates. But it’s not clear how much that will prompt a new surge in imports.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson and Lazaro Gamio

item.title: States Move to Limit Access to H.I.V. Treatment
item.description: Citing rising costs and shortfalls in federal support, about 20 states are toughening eligibility requirements for patients in drug assistance programs.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli
item.media:description: This week, thousands of Floridians were cut off from benefits that make H.I.V. medications more affordable. The medication Biktarvy was also dropped from Florida’s formulary.

item.title: Tariffs Force Down Heavy Equipment Sales and Jobs
item.description: An industry report shows high interest rates, fewer infrastructure projects and tariffs pressures have slowed growth and decreased jobs in the sector.
item.dc:creator: Farah Stockman
item.media:description: Heavy equipment manufacturers are producing less and employing fewer people than they did in 2022, according to a new report.

item.title: Airline Stocks Tumble as Iran War Expands Across the Middle East
item.description: Higher fuel prices and lower demand for international flights are expected to eat into airlines’ profits.
item.dc:creator: Kailyn Rhone and Niraj Chokshi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: The share prices of American Airlines, United Airlines and Delta Air Lines fell on Monday morning.

item.title: The Pentagon’s Favorite Tech Guy Is This Hawaiian Shirt-Wearing Founder
item.description: Palmer Luckey, who founded the defense tech start-up Anduril, has become the It Guy as President Trump aims to modernize the U.S. military with autonomous weapons.
item.dc:creator: Sheera Frenkel and Cade Metz
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces

item.title: The Costs of the Attacks on Iran
item.description: Rising oil prices, volatile stocks and potentially higher inflation are all weighing on investors’ minds as the military campaign continues.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Fears of a prolonged and wider war in the Middle East have gripped global markets.

item.title: Beyond a Trade War, China, Japan and Others in Asia Face a New Energy Threat
item.description: China and its neighbors scrambled to soften the blow of a disorderly trade war. Conflict in the Middle East now threatens to disrupt the oil imports that power their economies.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Stevenson, River Akira Davis and Meaghan Tobin
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A tanker passing through the waters of the Strait of Hormuz, last week. Shipping traffic through the narrow waterway on Iran’s southern border has slowed to a trickle.

item.title: Antitrust Trial to Challenge Live Nation’s Grip on the Music Industry
item.description: A jury will decide whether the music colossus, which owns venues, represents artists and operates Ticketmaster, is a monopoly, as the Justice Department contends.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario and David McCabe
item.media:description: Last year, Live Nation put on 55,000 events and sold 646 million tickets around the world. The government says the company has used its power to stifle competition and drive up ticket prices.

item.title: A Crisis in the Alps: Airbnb, Climate Change and Americans
item.description: The mountains’ resort towns have reached an inflection point, facing changes that threaten their cultures and even survival, as demand for short-term rentals reshapes the landscape.
item.dc:creator: Sophie Stuber

item.title: In Alysa Liu and Eileen Gu, China and America See a Mirror Image
item.description: The Olympic athletes are the subject of uncomfortable public comparisons that present online narratives that overlap more than both sides may realize.
item.dc:creator: Li Yuan
item.media:description: Ms. Liu during a training session at the Milan ice skating arena.

item.title: Global Markets Whipsaw After U.S.-Israel Attack on Iran
item.description: The strikes come at a precarious time for stocks, forcing investors to weigh the fallout of a potential prolonged disruption to oil supplies and logistics.
item.dc:creator: Joe Rennison, River Akira Davis and Eshe Nelson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries
item.media:credit: Karsten Moran for The New York Times
item.media:description: The attack on Iran has disrupted oil markets, shipping and supply chains, putting investors on edge.

item.title: Oil Prices Surge After Iran Attack
item.description: How long prices remain high will depend on what the United States, Israel and Iran do next.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries
item.media:description: An oil tanker anchored near Oman was ablaze on Sunday in this photo taken from a video.

item.title: Are ‘Bossware’ Tools Tracking You?
item.description: In recent years, the technologies used to surveil workers have become more sophisticated and widespread.
item.dc:creator: Lora Kelley

item.title: Kickstarter’s C.E.O. on Running a Fully Remote Company With a Four-Day Workweek
item.description: Everette Taylor took over Kickstarter at a low moment for the crowdfunding pioneer, which made its name with small projects but now attracts major multinationals and celebrities.
item.dc:creator: Jordyn Holman
item.media:credit: George Etheredge for The New York Times
item.media:description: Everette Taylor has changed the business model of the crowdfunding platform into what he calls Kickstarter 2.0.

item.title: 40 Years and 10,000 Broadcasts In, They’re Still Live on Air
item.description: Rick Ardon and Susannah Carr have been delivering the news together in Western Australia since 1985 — a Guinness World Record.
item.dc:creator: Katie Robertson and Matthew Abbott

item.title: Oil Shipments in Persian Gulf Already Disrupted by Iran Attack
item.description: Tankers have begun to steer clear of the region and the Strait of Hormuz, threatening to push up energy prices.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott and Peter Eavis
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Cargo ships and oil tankers in the Strait of Hormuz earlier this week.

item.title: Berkshire Hathaway Posts a Drop in Earnings in Buffett’s Last Year
item.description: In his first report as C.E.O., Gregory Abel, stuck to a straight commentary rather than Warren Buffett’s folksy tone. The lower earnings were largely driven by declines in the insurance business.
item.dc:creator: Michael J. de la Merced
item.media:description: Gregory E. Abel, right, the new chief executive of Berkshire Hathaway, with a Berkshire shareholder at the conglomerate’s annual meeting last year.

item.title: Banks Are Becoming Bulwarks for Vulnerable Seniors
item.description: Older Americans are losing billions of dollars annually to financial exploitation. Banks and investment firms are training employees to spot red flags and stop the transactions.
item.dc:creator: Paula Span

item.title: Lloyd Blankfein on Trump, Epstein and Life After Goldman Sachs
item.description: In a wide-ranging interview with Andrew Ross Sorkin, Goldman’s former C.E.O. discussed his life and new memoir.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin
item.media:credit: Oliver Farshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lloyd Blankfein, a former C.E.O. of Goldman Sachs, has published a memoir.

item.title: Netflix Lost Warner. Maybe That’s a Good Thing.
item.description: It is entirely possible, analysts say, that Netflix will be better off by bailing from its $83 billion deal with Warner Bros. Discovery.
item.dc:creator: Nicole Sperling
item.media:credit: Aleksey Kondratyev for The New York Times
item.media:description: Netflix’s offices on Sunset Boulevard in Los Angeles. “The truth is, Netflix is still producing more hits than everyone else combined,” one analyst said.

item.title: Warner Bros. Employees Glum About Prospect of Paramount Ownership
item.description: Employees at the company had started to warm to the idea of Netflix as its corporate owner. Now they face the prospect of major cuts under Paramount.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes and John Koblin
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Warner Bros. Studio in Burbank, California.

item.title: QAnon Faithful See Validation in the Epstein Files
item.description: The nearly decade-old conspiracy theory does not align neatly with the facts emerging from the documents. That does not seem to matter.
item.dc:creator: Tiffany Hsu and Stuart A. Thompson
item.media:credit: Samuel Corum for The New York Times
item.media:description: QAnon followers see validation of their theories in some elements of the Epstein files. But other parts of their canon, including the existence of a deep-state pedophilia conspiracy, are not backed up by the files.

item.title: 4 Ways to Enjoy Your Savings in Retirement Without Going Broke
item.description: Once paychecks stop, it can be frightening to shift out of the savings habit. But there are strategies to enjoy your nest egg and make it last.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Rabasca Roepe
item.media:credit: Amber Baesler for The New York Times
item.media:description: LeeAnn and Randy Smith had spent decades saving and investing, but were afraid they’d run out of money and have to rely on their children.

item.title: New Geothermal Technology Aims to Produce Clean Energy in Germany
item.description: Developers are using a new geothermal technology in Germany to produce clean energy.
item.dc:creator: Stanley Reed
item.media:credit: Roderick Aichinger for The New York Times
item.media:description: Detail of the teeth of a drill bit that will be used in the next phase of drilling.

item.title: In a Memoir Draft, Changpeng Zhao of Binance Details the Talks Leading to His Prison Time
item.description: In a draft of his memoir, Changpeng Zhao, the founder of Binance, laid out the secret negotiations that led to his imprisonment and a run-in with ICE.
item.dc:creator: David Yaffe-Bellany and Mattathias Schwartz
item.media:credit: Grant Hindsley for The New York Times
item.media:description: Binance’s founder, Changpeng Zhao, outside federal court in Seattle in 2024 at his sentencing for violating an anti-money-laundering law.

item.title: Justice Dept. Tries to Slow Down Legal Battle Over Tariff Refunds
item.description: Earlier, President Trump suggested on social media that he could try to persuade the Supreme Court to reconsider its rejection of his tariffs.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump rebuked the Supreme Court again on Friday.
